_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
**1. GHG data collection and carrier monitoring infrastructure**
The workspace must build or integrate a carrier-emissions data layer into its transport management system (TMS) or freight operations platform. Required data fields per ocean booking: vessel IMO number, CII rating (A–E), fuel type, estimated CO2e per TEU-km or tonne-km. Scope: all ocean-mode shipments across transatlantic, transpacific, and Europe–Asia lanes. Deadline: before mid-term measures enter into force (~2027); however, shipper-client scope 3 reporting obligations (CSRD, GHG Protocol) create a de facto earlier requirement (2025–2026 reporting years). Gap from baseline: most standard TMS platforms do not natively pull CII ratings; a carrier API or manual data-collection workflow must be designed.

**2. Carrier vetting and qualification criteria update**
The workspace's carrier qualification process should be updated to include: (a) confirmation of MARPOL Annex VI compliance (EEXI certificate, SEEMP Part III); (b) current CII rating; (c) fleet-level decarbonisation roadmap including alternative fuel commitments toward the 2030 ≥5% zero/near-zero fuel target. This applies particularly for lanes serving time-sensitive and high-value verticals (live events, fine art, film/TV equipment) where booking flexibility is limited and carrier substitution is costly. Deadline: update RFQ/tender templates within 60–90 days.

**3. Client-facing ESG reporting capability**
For shipper-clients subject to scope 3 or mandatory climate disclosure (luxury brands, studios with SEC/CSRD exposure), the workspace must be able to produce per-shipment GHG intensity estimates using carrier-supplied data and recognised methodologies (e.g., GLEC Framework, ISO 14083). This requires: training of operations staff in GHG calculation basics; contract clause with clients defining data-provision obligations; and a standard reporting template. Deadline: develop capability ahead of 2026 reporting cycle.

**4. Contract clause review**
Ocean freight contracts (master agreements, spot booking terms) should be reviewed to address: (a) carbon cost pass-through transparency obligations on carriers; (b) data-provision obligations (DCS/CII data within specified timeframes post-voyage); (c) substitution rights if a vessel is persistently rated D or E under CII. Deadline: next contract renewal cycle or within 90 days for key ocean lanes.

**5. Staff training**
Operations and account management staff responsible for ocean freight procurement across the workspace's verticals require baseline training in IMO GHG regulatory architecture: what EEXI, CII, and SEEMP mean operationally; how mid-term measures (fuel standard + GHG levy) will affect freight pricing; and how to communicate these to shipper clients. A brief (half-day) internal training module should be developed. Deadline: prior to mid-term measure entry into force; recommend completion within 6 months.

*Source: Resolution MEPC.377(80), IMO, 7 July 2023. https://wwwcdn.imo.org/localresources/en/KnowledgeCentre/IndexofIMOResolutions/MEPCDocuments/MEPC.377(80).pdf; IMO EEXI/CII FAQ, IMO. https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/eexi-cii-faq.aspx; IMO Mid- and Long-Term GHG Reduction Measures, IMO. https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/mid--and-long-term-ghg-reduction-measures.aspx*
